title: Kundalini and Faith: Not Religious Faith - The Faith That You'll Come Through This
meta_description: You're reading this, aren't you? Probably at 3 AM. Probably with your heart thumping so hard you can hear it in your ears. You're probably scared, confused
body:
Kundalini and Faith: Not Religious Faith - The Faith That You'll Come Through This

You're reading this, aren't you? Probably at 3 AM. Probably with your heart thumping so hard you can hear it in your ears. You're probably scared, confused, and feel completely alone. I know that feeling. I've been there. That raw, visceral terror that rips through you when you realize your reality isn't what you thought it was. When your body is doing things you can't explain, and your mind is a carnival of bizarre, intense experiences. Yeah, I know it. You're not crazy. You're not broken. You're just going through something most people can't even begin to imagine. And that's why you need a different kind of faith right now.

This isn't about God or gods, not in the way churches talk about them anyway. This isn't about dogma or scripture. This is about a deep, gut-level conviction that somehow, despite all the evidence screaming otherwise, you're going to make it through this. Because you will. I did. Others have. And you will too. It won't be easy. It's probably the hardest thing you'll ever face. But you've got to find that tiny spark of belief in yourself, in your own resilience, and fan it into a flame. Right?

The Crushing Weight of the Unknown

When Kundalini hits - or whatever you're calling this awakening, this spiritual emergency, this absolute meltdown - it's like someone pulled the rug out from under your entire life. Everything you thought was solid, every belief you held about yourself and the world, it just shatters. You might be experiencing intense heat, cold, vibrations, involuntary movements. You might be seeing things, hearing things, feeling things that defy logic. Sleep? Forget about it. Food? Who knows what your body wants anymore. Your relationships might be crumbling because how do you explain this to anyone? They'll think you're insane. They'll tell you to get help, and you'll go, and the doctors will just give you pills that make it worse or numb you out. Know what I mean?

That isolation is killer. It's the silent killer that whispers doubts in your ear, tells you you're the only one, that you're beyond help. But you're not. You're part of a small, strange, incredibly resilient club. A club you never asked to join, but here you are. And the first step to getting through this is acknowledging that you're in it. Not fighting it, not trying to cram it back into the box it exploded from. Just acknowledging. "Okay, this is happening. This is my reality right now."

When Your Body Betrays You

I remember the fear when my body started moving on its own. The tremors, the spasms, the feeling like electricity was coursing through my veins. It's terrifying because you feel like you've lost control. And in a way, you have. Your body is doing something ancient, something instinctual, something that's been dormant and is now waking up. It's clearing, it's reorganizing, it's doing what it needs to do. But your conscious mind, the one that likes to be in charge, freaks out.

You'll probably try to stop it. You'll clench your muscles, hold your breath, try to mentally suppress it. Don't. It's like trying to stop a sneeze once it's started. It's going to come out one way or another. And fighting it just creates more tension, more resistance, more suffering. It's like trying to hold a beach ball underwater. The harder you push, the more force it's going to come back with. Instead, try to soften. Try to breathe into it. I know it sounds impossible when you're being tossed around like a rag doll, but even a tiny bit of surrender can make a difference. Just a little "Okay, body, do your thing. I'm here." That's the beginning of this kind of faith. The faith that your own organism knows what it's doing, even if your brain doesn't.

The Mental Merry-Go-Round

And then there's the mind. Oh god, the mind. The looping thoughts, the existential dread, the sudden insights that feel like madness, the memories resurfacing from decades ago. It's a lot. You might feel like you're losing your grip on reality, like you're going insane. And honestly, it feels like that sometimes. But you're not. You're just experiencing a rapid-fire download, an accelerated processing of everything that's been stored away. It's like your internal hard drive is being defragmented and updated, all at once, without a pause button.

One of the best things you can do for your mind, beyond just allowing the thoughts to pass without judgment - which is a whole practice in itself - is to get it out. Write it down. Just dump it all onto paper. Don't edit, don't censor, just let it flow. This is where something like The Artist's Way Morning Pages Journal by Julia Cameron (paid link) can be really helpful, or just a good old Leuchtturm1917 Hardcover Dotted Notebook - A5 (paid link). It doesn't matter what it is, just get it out of your head and onto a page. It creates a little bit of distance, a little bit of breathing room. It lets you see the patterns, or just acknowledge the chaos, without it feeling quite so overwhelming inside your skull. Does that land?

Finding Your Anchor Points

When everything feels like it's spinning out of control, you need anchors. These aren't necessarily grand spiritual practices or exotic rituals. They're the simple, mundane things that remind you you're still here, still human, still connected to something. They're your lifelines.

  • Nature: Even if it's just sitting by a window and watching a tree, or stepping outside for five minutes, nature has a way of grounding you. It's slow, it's consistent, it doesn't judge. It just is.
  • Simple Movement: Walking, gentle stretching, anything that gets you out of your head and into your body in a non-threatening way. Don't push yourself. Just move for movement's sake.
  • Creative Expression: You don't have to be an artist. Drawing, doodling, coloring - anything that engages a different part of your brain and allows for non-verbal expression. A Mandala Coloring Book for Adults - Stress Relief (paid link) can be surprisingly calming. It's not about making something good; it's about making something.
  • Food and Water: I know, I know, sometimes eating feels impossible. But try to get something nourishing in you, even small amounts. Hydration is key. Your body is working overtime, it needs fuel.
  • Safe People: This is a tough one because most people won't get it. But if you have even one person - a partner, a sibling, a really good friend, a therapist who understands spiritual emergence - cling to them. Don't expect them to fix it, just let them be there. Their presence can be a powerful anchor.

These aren't cures, you know. They're just ways to help you stay present, to remind you that life is still happening around you, and that you're still a part of it, even if you feel completely disconnected. They're tiny acts of faith that say, "I'm going to keep showing up for myself."

The Faith of Patience and Persistence

Here's the thing: this isn't a sprint. It's a marathon through a desert, uphill, in a sandstorm. You're going to have days - weeks, months - where you feel like you're not making any progress, where you feel worse than ever. You'll question everything. You'll wonder if you're ever going to feel normal again. And that's okay. Those feelings are part of the process. Don't beat yourself up for having them.

But here's where that faith comes in. Not a belief that it'll all be over tomorrow. But a quiet, stubborn refusal to give up. A conviction that even though you can't see the path, you're still on one. You're moving. Even if it feels like you're crawling, you're still moving forward. This is where you learn what true grit means. You learn what it means to keep going when every cell in your body is screaming at you to stop. You learn what it means to trust that there's a deeper intelligence at play, even if it feels chaotic and terrifying.

I'll tell you what, I spent years in the thick of it. Years where I thought I'd lost my mind, lost my life. Years where I mourned the person I used to be. And then, slowly, painstakingly, things started to shift. Not back to "normal" - because that person is gone, you're becoming someone new - but to a place of integration, of acceptance, of a strange kind of peace. It's not the peace you had before; it's deeper, more earned, more real.

This process, sometimes called spiritual emergency, is something that's starting to get more recognition, thankfully. Books like Waking Up Is Hard to Do: Navigating Spiritual Emergence (paid link) can help you feel less alone and give you some context for what you're going through. It's not a quick fix, nothing is, but understanding that others have walked this path before you can be a huge comfort.

Releasing the Need to Understand Everything

Your mind wants answers. It wants to categorize, to label, to explain. And when it can't, it freaks out. The truth is, you won't understand everything that's happening right now. And that's okay. You don't need to. Think of it like this: if you were undergoing major surgery, you wouldn't necessarily understand every single cut, every stitch, every chemical reaction happening in your body. You'd just trust the surgeon to do their job. Right?

In this case, you're both the patient and the surgeon. Your deeper self, your inner wisdom, your body's innate intelligence - that's the surgeon. And it's doing a massive, complex operation on your entire being. Your job, as the patient, is to stay as calm as you can, to breathe, to allow, and to trust that the process has an intelligence to it, even if it feels random and violent. You don't have to grasp the mechanics of every single energy surge, every psychic download, every emotional purge. You just have to allow it to move through you.

This is a radical act of surrender, and it requires immense faith. Faith that there's a reason for this. Faith that you're being purified, upgraded, transformed. Faith that you're not just falling apart, but falling together in a new way. It's not easy. It's probably the hardest thing you'll ever do. But it's what's being asked of you right now.

The Eventual Integration - Not "Going Back"

You won't go back to who you were before this started. That person is gone. And honestly, you wouldn't want to be that person again, even if you could. This experience, as terrifying and painful as it is, is changing you at a cellular level. It's stripping away everything that isn't truly you, everything that's been holding you back. It's forcing you to confront your deepest fears, your shadow, your unhealed wounds.

What comes out on the other side is someone who is more authentic, more resilient, more compassionate, and far more awake. It's a new you, a more integrated you. It's a you that understands suffering on a level most people never will, and because of that, you'll have a depth of understanding and empathy that's truly rare.

The faith here is in that future self. The faith that you are evolving, even if it feels like you're dissolving. The faith that this intense, agonizing process has a purpose, even if that purpose isn't clear right now. It's the faith that you're not just surviving this, but you're going to come through it stronger, wiser, and more connected to who you truly are. And you will. You absolutely will. Keep breathing. Keep going. You're not alone in this dark night. I'm here. Others are here. And you will find your way through to the dawn. I promise you.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
